title: QLoRA
lesson:
QLoRA stands for Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation. It is one clever move stacked on top of another. First read LoRA, which freezes the big pretrained model and trains a tiny add-on (an adapter) instead of all the weights. QLoRA keeps that whole idea and adds a second saving: it shrinks the frozen model itself so it takes up far less memory while you train. The result is famous: in 2023 the QLoRA paper showed you could fine-tune a 65-billion-parameter model on a single 48 GB graphics card, something that previously needed a small cluster.

The problem QLoRA fixes

LoRA already makes the training cheap, because you only learn a small adapter. But there is a sneaky cost left over: you still have to hold the entire frozen base model in memory the whole time, just to push data through it. A 70-billion-parameter model stored in the normal 16-bit format eats roughly 140 GB of VRAM (the fast memory on a GPU). No single affordable card has that. So even though LoRA trains almost nothing, you still cannot fit the model on your hardware. QLoRA's job is to squeeze that frozen base down so it fits. In product terms, this is the difference between renting an eight-GPU server for hundreds of dollars a day and renting one card for a few dollars an hour — or even training overnight on a gaming PC you already own.

The packing analogy

Think of moving house. Your furniture is the model's weights. Normally each item travels in its own roomy crate (16 bits per number). Quantization — storing each number with fewer bits — is like vacuum-packing your clothes: same items, much less volume. QLoRA vacuum-packs the frozen base down to 4 bits per number, cutting its memory footprint to about a quarter. The clever part: you only unpack a box to its full size at the exact moment you need to use it, then re-pack it. So at any instant only a little is unpacked, and the bulk stays tightly compressed.

A second analogy: imagine a reference library. The huge encyclopedia (the base model) is printed in tiny, dense type to save shelf space — you can still read it, just barely. Your own sticky notes in the margins (the LoRA adapter) are written in big, clear handwriting because those are the only pages you actually edit. You never rewrite the encyclopedia; you only add and adjust the notes.

The three tricks inside QLoRA

QLoRA is really three ideas working together:

  • 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4). A special 4-bit number format tuned for the bell-curve shape that model weights naturally follow. It packs them more accurately than a plain 4-bit format would.
  • Double quantization. Quantization needs little helper numbers (called scales) to undo the compression. QLoRA compresses those helper numbers too — a small extra saving on top.
  • Paged optimizers. During training, sudden memory spikes can crash the run. QLoRA spills the overflow to regular computer memory (like a phone offloading old apps to storage), so a spike does not kill the job.

Crucially, the LoRA adapter still trains in full 16-bit precision. So the part that learns stays sharp; only the frozen part that just sits there gets compressed. The 4-bit weights are unpacked on the fly to 16-bit for each calculation, used, and discarded — you never store the unpacked version. This is why QLoRA is a touch slower than plain LoRA: that constant unpack-and-repack work is real, even if it is cheap.

What it looks like in code

With Hugging Face, you load the base model in 4 bits, then wrap it with a normal LoRA config:

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",        # NormalFloat 4-bit
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,   # compress the scales too
)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B", quantization_config=bnb)
model = get_peft_model(model, LoraConfig(r=16, lora_alpha=32))

Notice the adapter setup is identical to plain LoRA — the only new thing is the BitsAndBytesConfig that loads the base in 4 bits. Libraries like PEFT and Unsloth wrap all of this for you.

LoRAQLoRA
Base loaded in 16-bitBase loaded in 4-bit (NF4)
Trains a small adapterTrains the same small adapter
Needs ~140 GB for a 70B baseNeeds ~35 GB for a 70B base
Slightly faster per stepA bit slower (unpacking 4-bit costs time)

💡 QLoRA trades a little speed and a tiny accuracy loss for a huge memory saving. For most fine-tuning on a single consumer or rented GPU, that trade is well worth it.
mentalModel: A giant encyclopedia printed in tiny vacuum-packed type (the 4-bit frozen base) with your own big, clear sticky notes in the margins (the full-precision LoRA adapter). You never rewrite the book — you only add and tweak the notes, and the book barely takes up shelf space.
mistakes: Confusing QLoRA with plain Quantization for inference. QLoRA quantizes the base only to train cheaply. For fast serving afterward you usually merge or re-export the model — see GGUF Models. They are different stages, not the same thing.
Thinking the trained adapter is 4-bit too. The frozen base is 4-bit; the LoRA adapter still trains in 16-bit. That split is the whole point — the learning part stays sharp.

Skipping NF4 and double-quant settings. Loading 4-bit without bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4" and double quantization leaves accuracy and memory savings on the table. Use the recommended QLoRA defaults unless you have a reason not to.

Expecting zero quality loss. 4-bit compression is lossy, so there is a small quality dip versus 16-bit LoRA. It is usually tiny for focused tasks, but for very precision-sensitive work, test before shipping.

Forgetting that VRAM still scales with sequence length and batch size. QLoRA shrinks the weights, but long inputs and big batches still balloon the KV cache and activations. A 4-bit base does not make context length free.

Merging the adapter back into a still-quantized base. Merging into 4-bit weights can degrade quality. To merge cleanly, reload the base in full precision, merge the adapter there, then quantize the merged model if you want a small final file.
exercise:
Take a small open model (e.g. an 8B Llama or Qwen) and load it twice in a notebook: once normally, and once with a BitsAndBytesConfig using load_in_4bit=True and nf4. After each load, print the GPU memory used (e.g. torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1e9 in gigabytes). Write down both numbers and the ratio between them.

Stretch goal: Wrap the 4-bit model with a LoraConfig (r=16), call print_trainable_parameters(), and confirm two things at once: the base is now tiny in memory and only a fraction of a percent of parameters are trainable. Then fine-tune on ~50 example prompts and save just the adapter.
solution: Hint: Loading an 8B model in 16-bit takes roughly 16 GB of VRAM; in 4-bit NF4 it drops to roughly 5–6 GB — about a 3x saving. The exact ratio is a little under 4x because the helper scale numbers and the unpacking buffers add some overhead. After get_peft_model, print_trainable_parameters() will show well under 1% trainable, just like plain LoRA, because the adapter is identical — only the base storage changed. Save with model.save_pretrained('./adapter'), which writes only the small adapter weights, not the quantized base.
summary: QLoRA = quantization + LoRA. It loads the frozen base model in 4-bit (using NF4, double quantization, and paged optimizers) so it barely fits in memory, then trains a normal full-precision LoRA adapter on top. You get LoRA's cheap training plus a roughly 4x smaller memory footprint, letting you fine-tune large models on a single GPU — for a small, usually acceptable, quality cost.

keyTerms.def: Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation: fine-tuning where the frozen base model is stored in 4-bit while a normal LoRA adapter trains on top, slashing memory use.
Storing each number with fewer bits to save memory — like vacuum-packing data. QLoRA uses 4 bits per weight instead of 16.
A 4-bit number format tuned for the bell-curve spread of model weights, packing them more accurately than a plain 4-bit format.
Compressing the small helper numbers (scales) that quantization itself needs, for an extra bit of memory saving on top.
A trick that spills sudden memory spikes during training to regular computer memory so the run does not crash, like a phone offloading apps.
The fast memory on a graphics card. It is the usual bottleneck for fitting and training large models.
The small, trainable LoRA add-on. In QLoRA it stays in full 16-bit precision even though the base is 4-bit.
A compression that throws away a little information to save space, so the result is a close but not exact copy. 4-bit quantization is lossy.
